metadata.title: Power-Sharing, Presidential Style: Issue Salience and Portfolio Allocation in Multiparty Presidential Systems

abstract: Minority presidents form coalitions to secure legislative support. However, such support comes at the policy cost of delegating power to coalition partners. Given this dilemma, how do presidents decide on portfolio allocation? The argument is that presidents consider ministries' policy relevance and the characteristics of coalition parties when distributing positions. The president will allocate the most important ministries to aligned ministers (seeking policy control) and to the larger coalition parties (seeking legislative support), distributing the least policy-relevant ministries to clientelist parties. The article presents an original measure for policy salience based on topic modeling of presidential addresses and conditional logit models to test the hypotheses. The analysis consists of a comparative study of Brazil, Chile, and Colombia over 24 years. Results indicate that presidents use salience to sort ministries and decide portfolio allocation. This evidence for the president-specific salience argument shows the relevance of considering the qualitative aspects of portfolio allocation in multiparty presidential systems and how presidents share power with coalition partners without losing control over governmental decisions.

paragraphs: The combination of presidentialism and multiparty systems creates considerable challenges for governability in young democracies, especially in Latin America (Linz, 1990). The solution to the gridlock of multiparty presidentialism lies in coalition governments. Like their peers in parliamentary systems, minority presidents would seek to cooperate with the legislature through the formation of shared governments. This means exchanging cabinet positions for legislative support (Cheibub, 2007;Figueiredo & Limongi, 2001).

However, much of the literature on coalition governments in presidential systems focuses exclusively on the legislative dimension of their functioning. A second dimension is often dismissed: the governance dimension (Martínez-Gallardo & Schleiter, 2015). When presidents decide on coalition formation, they have in mind not only the potential for legislative support but also the policy costs of delegating power to coalition partners that, by definition, do not share the same preferences as the president. Thus, the main question is how presidents decide on portfolio allocation. This article argues that presidents will distribute part of the government to coalition partners while retaining another part to be handed over to ministers from their party and non-partisans (aligned ministers). However, this distribution is not carried out carelessly. The qualitative dimensions of ministries are considered-their policy salience or importance to the government agenda-so that the logic of portfolio allocation in coalitional presidentialism is the art of putting the right people in the right places. Thus, the president is expected first to allocate the ministries most important to their policy agenda to aligned ministers to keep control over governmental decisions. Second, considering the need to build legislative support, the president will share valuable posts with strong coalition parties that contribute the most to the coalition. Last, legislative parties are not all alike, and the president will allocate peripheral cabinet positions to clientelist parties that are rewarded with the perks of office.

To test this argument empirically, I propose a comparative analysis of Brazil, Chile, andColombia, between 1990 and2014. The choice is based on the 100% prevalence of coalition governments in these countries. This analysis's first contribution is constructing an original measure of portfolio salience based on the topic modeling of the presidential state of the union addresses. To code these texts, I use a supervised learning technique to identify the thematic emphases and their prevalence in texts. This measure is dynamic and country-specific, representing the first measure of portfolio salience in the comparative analysis of presidential systems. Using this indicator, I use conditional logit models to test the president-specific salience argument proposed in the article.

To further specify the analysis, robustness checks consider an alternative measure of portfolio salience, consisting of another original indicator based on the classification of the ministries responsible for policy coordination or the governmental core. Second, I further specify the meaning of "aligned ministers," analyzing the appointment of ministers from the president's party and non-partisans separately. I also present the results per country, identifying country-specific patterns that show presidents may emphasize different characteristics of parties in their calculus in different countries. Considering all these specifications, the results show evidence that the salience presidents attach to specific policy areas significantly impacts the allocation of ministries inside a coalition government. This evidence for the president-specific salience argument highlights the importance of considering the qualitative aspects of portfolio allocation in multiparty presidential systems, the central role that presidents play, and how they manage to share power with coalition partners without losing control over governmental decisions.

Taken together, the analyses offer three main contributions to the debate about how coalitions work in presidential systems. First, the article proposes a systematic comparative analysis of portfolio salience based on their importance to the president's policy agenda. The measure is original and innovative both in the study of presidential and parliamentary systems because it is dynamic and based on the agenda's content. Second, I show how presidents decide on portfolio allocation in multiparty presidential systems. They consider the qualitative attributes of the posts and the characteristics of the parties, prioritizing high-salience positions to aligned ministers and strong coalition parties. In contrast, clientelist parties are offered the office perks of cabinet participation in a secondary role. Third, I also show country-specific patterns and how the different characteristics of the choices (parties) matter in different countries. In sum, the article shows important differences in how presidents and parties coalesce, considering the institutional design of presidentialism, the central role presidents play in portfolio allocation, and country-specific political contexts.

The remainder of the article is organized as follows: the next section presents the literature on portfolio allocation, and then the following section develops the argument about who gets what in coalitional presidentialism. The fourth section offers an original measure of portfolio salience based on topic classification of the state of the union addresses. The fifth section presents the effect of portfolio salience and the characteristics of coalition parties on the president's allocation decision. The fifth section presents the robustness checks, focusing on the classification of core ministries as an alternative measure of portfolio salience and distinguishes the appointment of ministers from the president's party and no-partisans. The sixth section explores country-specific patterns. Last, there is a conclusion.

sections.paper_id: Batista - 2023 - Power-Sharing, Presidential Style Issue Salience and Portfolio Allocation in Multiparty Presidentia
paragraphs: Coalition politics is a central aspect of how democratic governments work. It defines who will be responsible for making policy decisions. For this reason, there is so much attention to the topic with a focus on who gets what in portfolio allocation. There are two main ways to analyze power distribution in coalition governments. One explores quantitative aspects of the distribution of posts, emphasizing the fair sharing of a prize (Gamson, 1961;Browne & Franklin, 1973) or the formateur advantage (Baron & Ferejohn, 1989). The second explores the qualitative aspects and how portfolio allocation connects with policymaking. This article contributes to the second approach.

The emphasis on what happens after portfolio allocation or how governments actually govern (Laver & Shepsle, 1990) makes the allocation of ministries strategic from a policy influence perspective. In a coalition government, where multiple parties and therefore multiple preferences are represented, who controls which specific ministry becomes critical. Not only how many posts a party gets is relevant, but which ministries and their policy attributes (Laver & Shesple, 1990). For this argument, portfolio salience or their qualitative aspects becomes fundamental.

The issue of portfolio salience was initially explored by assigning weights to ministries based on the emphasis that the policy issue they control receive from political parties in their platforms (Laver & Hunt, 1992;Druckman & Roberts, 2008;Warwick & Druckman, 2001;2006). However, this only partially explores the qualitative aspect of portfolio allocation, emphasizing the portfolio salience-weighted share each party gets. Back, Debus, & Dumont (2011) address this issue by analyzing each post's allocation decision as a discrete choice with all coalition parties as the allocation options. In this approach, the salience of each ministry for each party is taken into account, and the conclusion is that the parties that most emphasize the ministry's policy issue in their platforms tend to receive this post in portfolio allocation.

Another aspect important to the qualitative portfolio allocation approach is to explore portfolio salience and the ideological position of parties jointly. According to Greene & Jensen (2018), while the salience aspect began to be effectively examined in the study of portfolio allocation, the issue of divergence of preferences between the parties that make up the coalition was relegated to studies on ex-post controls between coalition partners such as coalition agreements, junior ministers, or legislative committees (Strom & Muller, 2000;Thies, 2001;Martin & Vanberg, 2011). Thus, for the analysis of portfolio allocation to be complete, it is necessary to analyze both the value attributed to the different ministries and who the parties in the coalition are and the divergence of preferences between them. The conclusion is that parties tend to receive the ministries they attribute the greatest prominence to. Still, the probability of receiving these ministries decreases the greater the ideological distance of that party in relation to the coalition median (Greene & Jensen, 2018).

Despite these many advances, this literature is still dominated by studies on parliamentary systems where coalition parties have joint responsibility for the government's survival. In presidential systems, it has also been identified that minority presidents seek, in the formation of coalitions, a way out of minority governments (Cheibub, 2007). Like their peers in parliamentary systems, presidents would allocate ministries to partner parties in order to build support for their legislative agenda (Amorim Neto, 2006;Cheibub, 2007). However, some differences in the institutional design of presidentialism make it impossible to simply translate the arguments and empirical findings reached in parliamentary systems.

In presidentialism, the president has a fixed term and does not depend directly on the coalition for the government's survival, although a legislative shield is an important factor in impeachment processes. In addition, presidents have constitutional powers of appointment for cabinet positions, being able to appoint and remove at any time during their term of office. This puts the president in the role of a natural coalition formateur where the president, and their party, cannot be left out of the government.

Presidents have various legislative powers, such as legislative initiative, veto, and budget control, which place them in a privileged position vis-à-vis the legislature and also in setting the agenda (Shugart & Carey, 1992).

In short, presidentialism imposes a hierarchical structure, with the president at the top, which is not observed in parliamentary systems (Shugart & Carey, 1992). However, these powers are not absolute. Legislative parties are still the critical instance in deciding the legislative success rate of the president's agenda. In this way, parties maintain strong bargaining power to counteract the powers of presidents so that coalition-building remains largely an exchange of ministries for legislative support (Figueiredo & Limongi, 2001;Amorim Neto, 2006).

Research on coalition politics in presidential systems shows some effects of these differences compared to parliamentary systems. Focusing on the quantitative dimension of portfolio allocation, Amorim Neto (2006) develops a Gamsonian analysis of the correlation between seats and ministries, proposing the coalescence rate as an aggregate measure. The results show that these rates are much lower than in parliamentary systems. Altman (2000) joins the argument of formal models, analyzing the formateur advantage in presidentialism. As natural formateurs, presidents tend to allocate a disproportionate number of ministries to their party (Altman, 2000).

Exploring the central role of presidents and the different motivations of political parties in presidential systems, the executive toolbox argument predicts that portfolio allocation is just one of the tools available to the president to build political support. Given that parties are not usually identified as programmatic, there is also the possibility of building political support using tools that appeal to the particularistic motivations of parties, especially the allocation of budget resources to electoral strongholds (Raile, Pereira & Power, 2011;Chaisty, Cheeseman & Power, 2018). Martinez-Gallardo & Schleiter (2015) explore how the governance dimension affects the distribution of ministries as a form of ex-ante control. The focus is on distributing ministries to members of the president's party or non-partisan ministers to minimize agency losses. The authors emphasize the principal-agent problem since presidents delegate power to cabinet ministers that may act against the president's preferences. They show that the president appoints non-partisans to minimize agency losses. However, they only analyze single-party governments.

Exploring coalition governments, Camerlo & Martínez-Gallardo (2017) argue that the president can allocate ministries by implementing a unilateral or cooperation strategy. The unilateral strategy indicates the inclusion of cabinet ministers whom the president trusts. The variation in these strategies will be contingent on the president's political and institutional strength. However, even though these contributions consider the president's need to control their agents when delegating power in government, they do not consider how portfolio salience matters to the rational allocation of portfolios. This article seeks to contribute to this literature on coalition politics in presidential systems, considering how the qualitative attributes of ministries matter for the president's decision on who will get what in portfolio allocation.

As natural formateurs, presidents tend to allocate a disproportionate number of ministries to their party (Altman, 2000).

Exploring the central role of presidents and the different motivations of political parties in presidential systems, the executive toolbox argument predicts that portfolio allocation is just one of the tools available to the president to build political support. Given that parties are not usually identified as programmatic, there is also the possibility of building political support using tools that appeal to the particularistic motivations of parties, especially the allocation of budget resources to electoral strongholds (Raile, Pereira & Power, 2011;Chaisty, Cheeseman & Power, 2018). Martinez-Gallardo & Schleiter (2015) explore how the governance dimension affects the distribution of ministries as a form of ex-ante control. The focus is on distributing ministries to members of the president's party or non-partisan ministers to minimize agency losses. The authors emphasize the principal-agent problem since presidents delegate power to cabinet ministers that may act against the president's preferences. They show that the president appoints non-partisans to minimize agency losses. However, they only analyze single-party governments.

Exploring coalition governments, Camerlo & Martínez-Gallardo (2017) argue that the president can allocate ministries by implementing a unilateral or cooperation strategy. The unilateral strategy indicates the inclusion of cabinet ministers whom the president trusts. The variation in these strategies will be contingent on the president's political and institutional strength. However, even though these contributions consider the president's need to control their agents when delegating power in government, they do not consider how portfolio salience matters to the rational allocation of portfolios. This article seeks to contribute to this literature on coalition politics in presidential systems, considering how the qualitative attributes of ministries matter for the president's decision on who will get what in portfolio allocation.

sections.paper_id: Batista - 2023 - Power-Sharing, Presidential Style Issue Salience and Portfolio Allocation in Multiparty Presidentia
sections.header_raw: How do Presidents Decide on Who Gets What in Coalition Governments?
sections.header_norm: how do presidents decide on who gets what in coalition governments
paragraphs: Coalition bargaining in presidential systems involves mainly two players-the president and the legislative parties. Coalition formation occurs through the distribution of ministries as a form of power-sharing. However, building legislative support is not the only political use of ministries. These are the primary loci of decision-making in the executive branch and can be used to formulate policies aligned with the president's preferences. This comes down to ministries performing these two tasks at the same time. In sum, portfolio allocation is both a matter of sharing office and influencing policy.

Given the institutional context of presidentialism, the composition of the government is primarily a choice of the president and not a horizontal bargain between the coalition parties. Presidential discretion matters (Chaisty, Cheeseman & Power, 2018). However, this choice is not without constraints since the coalition's purpose is to build political support to pass a legislative agenda. Assuming their central role in coalition formation, the president then seeks to maximize their utility, considering simultaneously the need to build legislative support and keep government decisions, for which they will be held accountable, under control. Therefore, the choice of who controls which ministry becomes vital, and the policy salience of the ministries 1 is fundamental. I propose the president-specific salience argument to understand how presidents decide on portfolio allocation. This means that the president will consider the policy salience of the specific ministry to their agenda and the parties' characteristics and decide who gets what in portfolio allocation.

Portfolio allocation can then be analyzed as a discrete choice that the president makes between the different parties that make up the coalition to occupy each specific ministry. The distribution of each post is the objective, and the different parties in the coalition are the alternatives. This choice is made based on the characteristics of the alternatives (parties) and the post. The central party characteristics for the argument are the alignment or divergence of preferences, the size of the contribution to the coalition, and the parties' motivation.

Considering the alignment or divergence of preferences, I analyze the extent to which presidents allocate ministries to aligned ministers, considering aligned ministers from the president's party and non-partisan ministers. When the president allocates a ministry to an aligned minister, they prioritize the goal of policy control. Examining the extent to which presidents centralize power when appointing aligned ministers dialogs with the argument of Greene & Jensen (2018) that it is necessary to include position or the actors' preferences in the discussion. In a presidential system, the proximity or divergence of preferences must be in reference to the position of the president. The president is expected to seek to retain power and appoint aligned ministers to the ministries most important to their policy agenda. Controlling the most important ministries is a way of maintaining control over the major government decisions. A positive relationship is expected between portfolio salience and the chance of allocation to aligned ministers. This hypothesis emphasizes the "presidential" part of the explanation about portfolio allocation in multiparty presidential systems.
H1: The greater the portfolio salience, the greater the probability of allocation to aligned ministers.

However, even though the president is at the top of the decision-making hierarchy in presidential systems, their power to act unilaterally is restrained by the need to build legislative support. The main feature in the literature on portfolio allocation is the party's contribution to the coalition in terms of seats in the legislature. It is expected that the larger the party, the larger its bargain power in the portfolio allocation game. Parties that contribute with a large seat share are in the position to demand not only cabinet positions but also important or high-salience posts, given their fundamental role in providing legislative support to the governmental agenda. This hypothesis emphasizes the legislative side of the coalition bargain. In the exchange of cabinet positions for legislative support, coalition parties can extract salient cabinet positions, depending on the size of their contribution to sustaining the government. This hypothesis advances in the debate dominated by purely office-seeking arguments, showing that coalition partners care not only about getting a sizable share of cabinet positions but also about getting the important or salient posts. This hypothesis emphasizes the "coalitional" part of the explanation about portfolio allocation in multiparty presidential systems.
H2: the greater the party's legislative contribution to the coalition, the more likely that party will receive a highsalience post.

Another characteristic of the parties, not yet explored in studies on portfolio allocation but theoretically relevant in contexts with few programmatic parties, is the party's motivations. Legislative parties are not all alike. Some parties may be programmatic and care about policy, but some have clientelist linkages with voters. The executive toolbox argument points to other tools that presidents can use to build political support, including particularistic resource allocation (Raile, Pereira & Power, 2011;Chaisty, Cheeseman & Power, 2018). These different tools can work as complements or substitutes to cabinet positions in securing the parties' legislative support. The argument is that when bargaining with clientelist parties, the president may secure legislative support in exchange for peripheral cabinet positions that may not be central to policymaking but still offer some perks of office, such as electoral visibility and control over particularistic resource allocation.
H3: the greater the party's level of clientelism, the less likely that party will receive a high-salience post.

In sum, the argument of this article contributes to the literature on portfolio allocation in multiparty presidential systems in three ways. First, it considers that portfolio allocation is both about office and policy. Most of the literature on portfolio allocation explores only the exchange of cabinet positions for legislative support, ignoring that these cabinet positions are fundamental for policymaking. Second, the argument puts the president at the center of the decision-making while considering coalition parties' bargaining power. For institutional reasons, portfolio allocation in presidential systems is not a bargain among equals. That is why portfolio salience is evaluated as a function of the president's policy agenda. However, the president cannot withhold important cabinet positions while excluding strong partners from policymaking. Third, the argument provides an explanation that combines the ministry's attributes and the parties' characteristics to explain who gets what. The next section shows the president-specific salience measure, and the following sections show the effect of portfolio salience and parties' characteristics on portfolio allocation decisions.

sections.paper_id: Batista - 2023 - Power-Sharing, Presidential Style Issue Salience and Portfolio Allocation in Multiparty Presidentia
sections.header_raw: Measuring Portfolio Salience Using State of the Union Addresses
sections.header_norm: measuring portfolio salience using state of the union addresses
paragraphs: This article presents an original measure of portfolio salience based on the emphasis placed on ministerial themes in the presidents' addresses. Presidential speeches, commonly called the State of the Union Address, are documents formulated by presidents for the legislatures, indicating the main points of their legislative agenda for that year. 2 These speeches are especially relevant to the issue discussed here because they indicate the topics and, therefore, ministries most important to the president's agenda. These are an expressed agenda that indicates government priorities. Raw data on presidential speeches come from Arnold, Doyle & Wiesehomeier (2017). Topic classification of these speeches to recover a measure of issue salience is first presented here.

The approach to retrieve issue salience information from these presidential addresses is one of a supervised machine learning nature. A supervised learning method is appropriate for a classification task where known categories are applied, such as the one implemented here. The speeches were pre-processed for analysis, and the text was broken down into sentences. Each country was considered a separate corpus. This means that the speeches from each country were analyzed as individual projects. This is relevant because of differences in language between countries and specific policies, problems, and events mentioned in the speeches. This approach's goal was to implement an automatized form of analysis that can travel virtually to every other country with a state of the union address while keeping relevant information regarding the policy context of each country. Brazil's speeches are the shortest, with a corpus of 3.322 sentences; Colombia's corpus has 6.163; and Chile's corpus has 15.361.

The codification scheme includes 20 substantive topics. These topics come from juxtaposing the most frequent ministries in the three countries analyzed. One additional code is included to capture the part of the speech that does not show any policy content. These are mentions and acknowledgments to Congress, political supporters, or even the president's family and friends. Since this information is not policy-relevant, this code captures this information, and the sentences are later excluded from the analysis. The 20 substantive topics reflect the most relevant issues and relate directly to the ministries. 3 A supervised machine learning algorithm works as follows: a human codes a sample that will be used to "teach" the algorithm to code the rest of the corpus. This "training set" size can vary according to the research design. Following Collingwood & Wilkerson (2012), who indicate at least 100 examples of each code, random samples of 2.500 sentences for each country were handcoded. The entire corpus of Brazil was hand-coded to test different algorithms and choose the most efficient ones. The results of these tests are shown in the supplementary material.

Once the training and test steps are completed, the next step is to use the model to code unclassified text. There are several different algorithms to produce this model. The approach was to use four different algorithms to increase leverage. The idea is that every algorithm has an error rate. Using more than one algorithm makes it possible to calculate ensemble statistics. A majority rule was implemented, meaning that this code is accepted when at least three of the four algorithms agree on assigning a code to a sentence. When there is disagreement on the code to assign, additional hand-coding is required to "resolve the dispute." This procedure is time-consuming but increases the precision of the final codification. The four algorithms chosen based on their accuracy were: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Maximum Entropy, Random Forest, and Boosting (Jurka, Collingwood, Boydstun, Grossman & van Atteveldt 2013). 4 After the classification procedure, each topic's proportion was assigned to the ministries, and the proportions were used as salience weight.

To validate the results, I use two different strategies. First, I present the second measure of portfolio importance based on the classification of "core ministries," or the ministries responsible for policy coordination. I then check the difference of means between the core ministries and the non-core ministries. The ministries considered the governmental core were classified based on the core executive literature (Elgie, 2011), mainly the functional version establishing which bodies play a role in government coordination. The core ministries are Finance, Planning and Administration, Chief of Staff, Foreign Relations, Interior, Justice, and Defense. This classification of the ministries composing the governmental core is static, defined beforehand, and not subject to strategic manipulation by the president. For these reasons, this classification serves as a check on the more variable measure based on issue salience. T-Test results are presented in the supplementary material.

In the second validation strategy, I correlate portfolio salience with Brazil's ministries' actual policy agenda. This actual agenda is the product of governmental action and can be observed as the policies the government presented to the legislature. Since one or more ministers sign each bill, it is possible to measure the ministries' policy activity or participation in the government's policy agenda. Correlating the emphasis of each ministry's theme in the state of the union address with the participation of that ministry in the actual governmental agenda also indicates that these speeches are not just "cheap talk." The correlation shows a positive relationship between the expressed and actual agendas and is also presented in the supplementary material. From Figures 1 to 3, it is possible to notice that issue salience varies according to country and time. In Brazil, macroeconomics is the most salient issue by far, even considering that presidents from the center-right and left have governed the country during this period. Exogenous pressures can explain this since the country has struggled with high inflation and unemployment for a long time. On the other hand, the most salient issue in Colombia is Law and Crime, followed by Defense, which is not a salient issue in the other countries because of the absence of war or foreign threats.

In Colombia's case, the explanation for the high salience of these issues related to justice and violence is the armed conflict against the FARC (Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia). In Chile, the most salient issue is also Law and Crime, but for different reasons. Chilean presidents emphasize this topic because of the salience of the civic-military relations and human rights resulting from the transition process from the dictatorship. The second most salient issue is Education, reflecting its relevance in Chilean society due to the mainly privatized educational system.

Besides the variation between countries, there is also variation within each country, as shown by the vertical bars. This means substantive variation in issue salience in time or between different presidents. For example, in Brazil, while Cardoso (PSDB, center-right) focused on state reform issues, Lula (PT, left) focused on social development. This shows that presidents of different ideologies also tend to emphasize different issues. However, presidents from the same political party can also emphasize different issues, such as Rousseff (PT, left) did, emphasizing infra-structure even though she was elected from the same leftist party as her predecessor, who emphasized social development.

These results show that some issues are salient in the agenda because of external imposition, such as an economic crisis or external shock. In other cases, issues are relevant because of conflicts and policies related to the domestic environment. However, structural factors are relevant, but the president's ideology is also important to explain why some issues are more salient than others at a specific point in time. Last, the individual also matters, and presidents sharing the same ideological position may emphasize different policies. These variations between and within countries are why analyzing issue salience from a dynamic and country-specific perspective is necessary.

On the other hand, some issues are only rarely emphasized, and these are very similar in all three countries, such as tourism and sports. Even though all three countries created a portfolio for these issues at some point, these are consistently the least salient ones indicating that these are cabinet positions with very low policy value compared to the others. In a nutshell, the conclusion is that the Ministry of Finance, usually responsible for macroeconomic policy, and the Ministry of Tourism cannot be analyzed as if having the same value, with the risk of making very mistaken judgments about coalition politics. In sum, some issues are high-valued, while others are low-valued. This article argues that these qualitative differences in value or salience between ministries are fundamental to the president's decision on who gets what in portfolio allocation.

In Brazil, macroeconomics is the most salient issue by far, even considering that presidents from the center-right and left have governed the country during this period. On the other hand, the most salient issue in Colombia is Law and Crime, followed by Defense, which is not a salient issue in the other countries because of the absence of war or foreign threats.

In Colombia's case, the explanation for the high salience of these issues related to justice and violence is the armed conflict against the FARC (Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia). Chilean presidents emphasize this topic because of the salience of the civic-military relations and human rights resulting from the transition process from the dictatorship. The second most salient issue is Education, reflecting its relevance in Chilean society due to the mainly privatized educational system.

Besides the variation between countries, there is also variation within each country, as shown by the vertical bars. This means substantive variation in issue salience in time or between different presidents. For example, in Brazil, while Cardoso (PSDB, center-right) focused on state reform issues, Lula (PT, left) focused on social development. This shows that presidents of different ideologies also tend to emphasize different issues. However, presidents from the same political party can also emphasize different issues, such as Rousseff (PT, left) did, emphasizing infra-structure even though she was elected from the same leftist party as her predecessor, who emphasized social development.

These results show that some issues are salient in the agenda because of external imposition, such as an economic crisis or external shock. In other cases, issues are relevant because of conflicts and policies related to the domestic environment. However, structural factors are relevant, but the president's ideology is also important to explain why some issues are more salient than others at a specific point in time. Last, the individual also matters, and presidents sharing the same ideological position may emphasize different policies. On the other hand, some issues are only rarely emphasized, and these are very similar in all three countries, such as tourism and sports. sections.text_with_footnotes:
This article presents an original measure of portfolio salience based on the emphasis placed on ministerial themes in the presidents' addresses. Presidential speeches, commonly called the State of the Union Address, are documents formulated by presidents for the legislatures, indicating the main points of their legislative agenda for that year. 2 These speeches are especially relevant to the issue discussed here because they indicate the topics and, therefore, ministries most important to the president's agenda. These are an expressed agenda that indicates government priorities. Raw data on presidential speeches come from Arnold, Doyle & Wiesehomeier (2017). Topic classification of these speeches to recover a measure of issue salience is first presented here.

The approach to retrieve issue salience information from these presidential addresses is one of a supervised machine learning nature. A supervised learning method is appropriate for a classification task where known categories are applied, such as the one implemented here. The speeches were pre-processed for analysis, and the text was broken down into sentences. Each country was considered a separate corpus. This means that the speeches from each country were analyzed as individual projects. This is relevant because of differences in language between countries and specific policies, problems, and events mentioned in the speeches. This approach's goal was to implement an automatized form of analysis that can travel virtually to every other country with a state of the union address while keeping relevant information regarding the policy context of each country. Brazil's speeches are the shortest, with a corpus of 3.322 sentences; Colombia's corpus has 6.163; and Chile's corpus has 15.361.

The codification scheme includes 20 substantive topics. These topics come from juxtaposing the most frequent ministries in the three countries analyzed. One additional code is included to capture the part of the speech that does not show any policy content. These are mentions and acknowledgments to Congress, political supporters, or even the president's family and friends. Since this information is not policy-relevant, this code captures this information, and the sentences are later excluded from the analysis. The 20 substantive topics reflect the most relevant issues and relate directly to the ministries. 3 A supervised machine learning algorithm works as follows: a human codes a sample that will be used to "teach" the algorithm to code the rest of the corpus. This "training set" size can vary according to the research design. Following Collingwood & Wilkerson (2012), who indicate at least 100 examples of each code, random samples of 2.500 sentences for each country were handcoded. The entire corpus of Brazil was hand-coded to test different algorithms and choose the most efficient ones. The results of these tests are shown in the supplementary material.

Once the training and test steps are completed, the next step is to use the model to code unclassified text. There are several different algorithms to produce this model. The approach was to use four different algorithms to increase leverage. The idea is that every algorithm has an error rate. Using more than one algorithm makes it possible to calculate ensemble statistics. A majority rule was implemented, meaning that this code is accepted when at least three of the four algorithms agree on assigning a code to a sentence. When there is disagreement on the code to assign, additional hand-coding is required to "resolve the dispute." This procedure is time-consuming but increases the precision of the final codification. The four algorithms chosen based on their accuracy were: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Maximum Entropy, Random Forest, and Boosting (Jurka, Collingwood, Boydstun, Grossman & van Atteveldt 2013). 4 After the classification procedure, each topic's proportion was assigned to the ministries, and the proportions were used as salience weight.

To validate the results, I use two different strategies. First, I present the second measure of portfolio importance based on the classification of "core ministries," or the ministries responsible for policy coordination. I then check the difference of means between the core ministries and the non-core ministries. The ministries considered the governmental core were classified based on the core executive literature (Elgie, 2011), mainly the functional version establishing which bodies play a role in government coordination. The core ministries are Finance, Planning and Administration, Chief of Staff, Foreign Relations, Interior, Justice, and Defense. For these reasons, this classification serves as a check on the more variable measure based on issue salience. T-Test results are presented in the supplementary material.

sections.paper_id: Batista - 2023 - Power-Sharing, Presidential Style Issue Salience and Portfolio Allocation in Multiparty Presidentia
sections.header_raw: Salience and Portfolio Allocation Strategies from a Comparative Perspective
sections.header_norm: salience and portfolio allocation strategies from a comparative perspective
paragraphs: The main goal of the analysis is to identify whether presidents' attention to specific policy issues significantly impacts portfolio allocation in a coalition government. To test this president-specific salience argument, I use the portfolio salience measure originally presented in the previous section and data on the ministers and their party affiliation that come from Camerlo & Martínez-Gallardo (2017).

I follow Back, Debus, & Dumont (2011) and use MacFadden's conditional logit approach (McFadden, 1973) to understand the president's decision on portfolio allocation. In this approach, each post is the unit of analysis, and the different parties in the coalition are the options of a discrete choice. Put differently, when deciding which party to designate to each ministry, the president has all coalition parties as options. Using a conditional logit, we can evaluate the role of choicespecific variables (party features) and also the role of features of each specific post (portfolio salience). The observation is then the post-party-cabinet pairing. The data has been collected considering a new "cabinet" and not a government formation episode as usual in studies on parliamentary systems because, in presidential systems, the president can appoint and remove ministers during the mandate. A new cabinet or a cabinet reshuffle is considered when the president changes the party composition of at least one cabinet post.

The first choice-specific feature is whether the minister is aligned with the president or not. The ministers considered to be aligned are those affiliated with the president's party or non-partisan ministers. The second choicespecific feature is the party's seat share which refers to the seats that the parties gained in the election to the lower chamber. The third choice-specific feature is the party's level of clientelism. The level of clientelism is measured as the answer to the question, "to what extent do the party and its candidates provide targeted and excludable (clientelist) goods and benefits-such as consumer goods, cash, or preferential access to government services-in an effort to keep and gain votes?" Both information comes from the V-Party Dataset (Lührmann et al., 2020).

The models reported in the main text include only the choice-specific variables and the post attribute (salience). In the supplementary material, I show the same models, including government-specific features such as popularity (Carlin et al., 2016), inflation as a measure of economic crises (World bank), and the legislative cohesion that measures unity in voting important bills (Coppedge et al., 2021). Results are identical to the restrained models reported in the text.

Table 1 shows the results of the conditional logit models. Model 1 shows the results for the choice-specific features. Models 2, 3, and 4 show the interaction terms with each choice-specific feature, showing the effect of the choice-specific features conditional on the portfolio salience. Considering first the party-specific variables, results in model 1 show that aligned ministers have an increased probability of receiving the post. This result is expected from the perspective that presidents will tend to retain power by appointing ministers they can trust to positions in the cabinet. The second choice-specific feature is the party's seat share in the lower house. This is a common variable to measure the party's strength or bargain power, considering their contribution to sustaining the coalition. The result shows that the greater the party's seat share, the greater the probability the party will receive the post. This result is consistent with most of the literature on portfolio allocation, which expects that the party's legislative support will be compensated with cabinet seats in the coalition game.

Last, the third choice-specific feature is the level of clientelism of the party. This aspect of parties is not usually explored because a common assumption in the coalition literature is that of the responsible party. However, in presidential systems, it is common to observe a particularistic orientation, especially since they are not directly responsible for the government's survival. Hence, this variable becomes crucial because it can indicate the value ministries have for that party or to what extent other tools such as particularistic resource allocation can be used to build legislative support (Raile, Pereira & Power, 2011;Chaisty, Cheeseman & Power, 2018). The coefficient in Table 1 shows that the greater the party's clientelism, the less likely that party will receive the post. This is the first analysis of the relationship between clientelism and portfolio allocation to the best of our knowledge.

The article's hypotheses concern the interaction terms in models 2, 3, and 4, which indicate the effect of the choice-specific features considering the attribute of the post in question. The attribute is, of course, portfolio salience. The president-specific salience argument is that in portfolio allocation, the president will seek to maximize their utility by balancing two different objectives: building legislative support and maintaining control over government decisions. The way to do this would be to rationally allocate ministries, choosing ministers accordingly to the policy-relevance of ministries for the government's agenda. The models consider the interaction between alignment, seat share, and clientelism with portfolio salience. 5 Model 2 shows the interaction between alignment and salience. In this way, preferences and salience are considered together. In Table 1, it is possible to see that the coefficient for the interaction between alignment and salience is positive and statistically significant. The greater the salience of the ministry, the greater the probability of it being allocated to a minister aligned with the president. Substantively, the increase of salience in one unity increases the chance of appointing an aligned minister by 28%. In practice, this means, for example, that the probability of appointment of an aligned minister for a ministry such as the Ministry of Finance in Brazil in 1995 (salience = 2.08) is 28% higher than for the Ministry of Industry in 1998 (salience = 1.02), all else held constant. This indicates evidence favorable to the first hypothesis that the president uses salience to sort ministries and designates the parties according to the proximity of preferences.

Alignment with the president is an important party feature because the institutional design of presidential systems puts the president at the top of the executive decision-making hierarchy. This includes deciding on portfolio allocation and pursuing control over policymaking. However, a second important feature of the political systems analyzed in this article is the coalitional aspect. Presidents share power to build legislative support. Model 3 shows that the coalition parties' bargain power matters. The coefficient is positive and statistically significant, indicating the increased probability of receiving a more salient post. The greater the party's contribution to the coalition, the greater the effect of portfolio salience on the probability of receiving the post. This result indicates that the parties most important to sustain the coalition, providing legislative support to the government's agenda, not only gain control of cabinet posts but get differential access to the most salient cabinet positions. This result shows evidence in favor of hypothesis 2. It is an important contribution to the literature because it indicates that coalition parties also care about policy in the portfolio allocation game and not just the sharing of an indistinguishable prize.

Alignment and seat share are important party features relevant to understanding how presidents and coalition parties share power. However, the explanation would be incomplete if it did not consider that parties are not all alike and that the level of clientelism may affect the presidential calculus on portfolio allocation. If clientelist parties care less about policy, it can be expected that the president will seek the legislative support of these parties and, in exchange, reward them with less relevant cabinet posts that still offer the perks of office. When appointing aligned ministers to policy-relevant posts, the president prioritizes the goal of policy control. When appointing large parties to policy-relevant posts, the president is concerned with building legislative support. When bargaining with clientelist parties, the president may pursue both goals simultaneously. Clientelist parties may provide the legislative support needed, and the president can still keep control over governmental decisions by delegating less policy-relevant posts. These peripheral posts are not central to policymaking but offer perks of office that can be valuable to clientelist parties. Results of model 4 show evidence in favor of this hypothesis. The coefficient is negative and statistically significant, indicating that more clientelist parties are less likely to get a high-salience post.

These results contribute to the study of coalition governments in presidential systems, providing evidence in favor of the president-specific salience argument. There is also convergence with studies on the qualitative portfolio allocation in parliamentary systems, especially Back, Debus, & Dumont (2011) and Greene & Jensen (2018), which show the effect of salience and position on bargaining between coalition parties about who gets what. The difference here is the focus on the role of the president and portfolio salience according to their policy agenda. In this way, it is possible to understand how presidents share power with coalition parties while retaining control over government decisions.

This indicates evidence favorable to the first hypothesis that the president uses salience to sort ministries and designates the parties according to the proximity of preferences.

Alignment with the president is an important party feature because the institutional design of presidential systems puts the president at the top of the executive decision-making hierarchy. This includes deciding on portfolio allocation and pursuing control over policymaking. However, a second important feature of the political systems analyzed in this article is the coalitional aspect. Presidents share power to build legislative support. Model 3 shows that the coalition parties' bargain power matters. The coefficient is positive and statistically significant, indicating the increased probability of receiving a more salient post. The greater the party's contribution to the coalition, the greater the effect of portfolio salience on the probability of receiving the post. This result indicates that the parties most important to sustain the coalition, providing legislative support to the government's agenda, not only gain control of cabinet posts but get differential access to the most salient cabinet positions. This result shows evidence in favor of hypothesis 2. It is an important contribution to the literature because it indicates that coalition parties also care about policy in the portfolio allocation game and not just the sharing of an indistinguishable prize.

Alignment and seat share are important party features relevant to understanding how presidents and coalition parties share power. However, the explanation would be incomplete if it did not consider that parties are not all alike and that the level of clientelism may affect the presidential calculus on portfolio allocation. If clientelist parties care less about policy, it can be expected that the president will seek the legislative support of these parties and, in exchange, reward them with less relevant cabinet posts that still offer the perks of office. When appointing aligned ministers to policy-relevant posts, the president prioritizes the goal of policy control. When appointing large parties to policy-relevant posts, the president is concerned with building legislative support. When bargaining with clientelist parties, the president may pursue both goals simultaneously. Clientelist parties may provide the legislative support needed, and the president can still keep control over governmental decisions by delegating less policy-relevant posts. These peripheral posts are not central to policymaking but offer perks of office that can be valuable to clientelist parties. Results of model 4 show evidence in favor of this hypothesis. The coefficient is negative and statistically significant, indicating that more clientelist parties are less likely to get a high-salience post.

These results contribute to the study of coalition governments in presidential systems, providing evidence in favor of the president-specific salience argument. There is also convergence with studies on the qualitative portfolio allocation in parliamentary systems, especially Back, Debus, & Dumont (2011) and Greene & Jensen (2018), which show the effect of salience and position on bargaining between coalition parties about who gets what. The difference here is the focus on the role of the president and portfolio salience according to their policy agenda. In this way, it is possible to understand how presidents share power with coalition parties while retaining control over government decisions.

sections.paper_id: Batista - 2023 - Power-Sharing, Presidential Style Issue Salience and Portfolio Allocation in Multiparty Presidentia
paragraphs: To check the robustness of the results presented in the previous section, I use two alternative specifications of the main variables. First, an alternative measure of portfolio salience, and second, an alternative measure of alignment. Table 2 shows the results of the same models presented in Table 1 but substituting the salience variable for the classification of "core" ministries discussed earlier.

Models 5, 6, and 7 in Table 2 show that the results remain stable as compared to the results in Table 1. Aligned ministers are more likely to be appointed to a governmental core ministry. These are the key cabinet seats, and a president concerned with policy control would have great incentives to keep these seats occupied by an ally. For example, when the president appoints an ally to the Ministry of Finance, they exert control over all other ministries' budgetary priorities, government expenditure, and public deficit. These are all outcomes that the president will be directly held accountable for but, due to power-sharing reasons, may not have strict control over.

Strong coalition parties also use their bargaining power when considering core ministries. The president may have incentives to retain core ministries by appointing aligned ministers, but coalition partners are also interested in influencing policymaking. The result in Table 2 indicates that in a situation where the president becomes dependent on coalition parties with enough seats to approve the governmental legislative agenda or, in a more extreme situation, sustain the government against an impeachment trial, the president will be willing to delegate core cabinet seats. The larger the seat share, the greater the probability of getting a governmental core post. In the same way, as in Table 1, clientelist parties have a decreased probability of gaining control of a governmental core ministry. This result shows that clientelist parties are included in the government in a peripheral position when considering policy control.

Table 3 presents the second robustness test and assesses to what extent the results remain stable when the classification of aligned ministers is disaggregated. For the argument, the president will seek to appoint aligned ministers to maintain control over government decisions. However, the classification includes ministers without party affiliation and those affiliated with the president's party. The results in Table 3 can answer whether the president actually differentiates ministers affiliated with their party from non-partisan ministers in coalition governments, prioritizing one of these types for the allocation of more important ministries.

Model 9 in Table 3 shows that the president prioritizes ministers affiliated with their party for the most salient ministries. The greater the salience of the post, the greater the probability of appointing a minister affiliated with the president's party. The result is not statistically significant when considering non-partisan ministers, indicating no systematic difference between non-partisan ministers and ministers affiliated with the other coalition parties. This result shows the prioritization of the president's party in coalition governments. In this case, the appointment of copartisans does not represent a possible agency loss but rather a better indication of the alignment of political and ideological preferences. Models 8, 10, and 11 are presented for the sake of comparison with the models shown in Table 1. Results remain identical when including this alternative measure of alignment.

Taken together, the empirical findings of these sections show that the policy salience of portfolios varies expressively and that a measure capable of capturing variance in time and between different countries is necessary to answer important questions about coalition governance. Second, presidents consider policy salience when deciding whom to appoint to a specific cabinet position. In other words, presidents use salience to sort ministries and decide to whom to delegate power. In sum, there are essential specificities in how and with whom presidents share power in coalition governments, and this article contributes to this literature. The next section further explores these results, showing country-specific patterns of the president's decision on who gets what.

sections.paper_id: Batista - 2023 - Power-Sharing, Presidential Style Issue Salience and Portfolio Allocation in Multiparty Presidentia
paragraphs: The previous sections' analyses show the effect of the variables considering all three countries in aggregate. This is important considering the comparative nature of the research presented in this article. However, it is possible to question whether the models have the same explanatory power in the countries separately or if the presidents in the different countries prioritize different party-specific The first way to analyze these country-specific patterns without losing the comparative perspective proposed in the article is to assess the level of concordance reached by the model in each country individually. This procedure is standard in studies that use conditional logit models. It indicates the probability that the prediction performed by the model goes in the same direction as the actual data (Back, Debus, & Dumont, 2011). In other words, concordance is the convergence between what the model predicted and which party actually received the ministry in the data. Table 4 shows the same models presented in Table 1 but performed with each country separately.

The results show that in the three countries, the concordance is reasonably high. Brazil has the highest concordance rate, Colombia second, and Chile last. However, the numbers are close, indicating homogeneity. Comparing the different models, the analyses of Brazil do not suggest that a party-specific feature substantively changes the correctly predicted rate. However, for Chile and Colombia, there is a slight but relevant improvement in the prediction rate when considering the model with the interactive term between seat share and salience. This result indicates that seat share might be a more relevant party feature in the presidential calculus on who gets what in portfolio allocation in these countries.

Figure 4 shows the results of the same models presented in Table 1, but separately per country. Quantitative comparative analysis is important to show general patterns. This article's main goal was to understand how presidents share power with coalition parties and if there is evidence in favor of the president-specific salience argument presented here. However, a factor may significantly affect the allocation choice in the general model but not explain the allocation decision in specific countries. Therefore, this analysis is important to identify possible country-specific patterns.

Analyzing the models separately per country shows important results. First, considering the party-specific features, alignment has a positive and statistically significant effect in all three countries. This indicates that presidential discretion is important and aligned ministers are more likely to receive the post, regardless of these countries' specific institutional design and political context. Second, seat share continues to have a positive and statistically significant effect in the separate models. This indicates that the greater the party's seat share, the more likely this party will get the post. This result indicates that coalition parties use their bargaining power to control cabinet seats in all three countries analyzed. This result is important because it converges with the almost universal claim that contribution to the coalition affects the ability to extract gains in the coalition formation game. This gain usually comes in the form of cabinet posts. Third, clientelism shows the first heterogeneous result when comparing the three countries. It has a positive effect in Brazil, but not in the other two countries. This means that in Brazil, clientelist parties demand cabinet seats, affecting the presidential calculus. This result is contrary to the expectation that clientelist parties' support could be achieved through means other than sharing cabinet positions (Raile, Pereira & Power, 2011;Chaisty, Cheeseman & Power, 2018). This result might be explained by the fact that Brazil is an outlier in terms of party fragmentation and clientelist parties. This interpretation points to a research agenda about how unconstrained the president's choice really is on forming coalitions and sharing power given the party system's structure. However, this result is still consistent with the expectations of this article because the interpretation of the simple slope in models with interaction terms is the effect of the variable when the interacted variable assumes the value of zero. Put in another words, the effect of clientelism is positive for getting a post of zero salience. Therefore, clientelist parties in Brazil are assuming a peripheral position in portfolio allocation with regard to policy influence.

Concerning the interactive terms, in Brazil, the main factor driving the president's choice on who gets the most important cabinet positions is alignment and clientelism. When interacted with the portfolio salience, alignment shows positive and statistically significant result, indicating that Brazilian presidents are concerned with policy control. Conversely, clientelism shows negative and statistically significant effect, indicating that the president allocates the least important posts to these parties. Seat share does not show statistically significant results, indicating that the parties' bargain power is not the main driver of the president's decision on who gets the most policy-relevant cabinet seats. Considering Chile, the only statistically significant result is the interaction of salience with seat share, indicating that in this country, the bargaining power of coalition parties is the main driver in the allocation of the most important posts. In Colombia, the coalition parties' bargaining power is also important. The interaction of salience with seat share is positive and statistically significant, indicating that the coalition's pressure for power-sharing is fundamental for the president's choice in this country. The interaction of salience with clientelism is also statistically significant, but the coefficient is positive, indicating that Colombian presidents delegate policyrelevant cabinet positions to clientelist parties, contrary to the expectations of this article.

In sum, these results indicate that in Brazil, the president prioritizes their need for policy control, appointing aligned ministers and non-clientelist parties to policyrelevant cabinet positions. In Chile and Colombia, the main concern is the need for legislative support, with the president prioritizing parties that contribute the most to the coalition and also clientelist parties in the case of Colombia. This analysis disaggregated per country shows that there are points of convergence and important country-specific patterns that must be considered when analyzing coalition politics in these countries. Last, these results indicate that coalition politics in Brazil has its specificities. Considering that most of the literature on multiparty presidentialism uses Brazil as a case study, the results presented here indicate that generalizing from these case studies should be taken with a grain of salt.

sections.paper_id: Batista - 2023 - Power-Sharing, Presidential Style Issue Salience and Portfolio Allocation in Multiparty Presidentia
paragraphs: This article aimed to identify how presidents share power in multiparty governments. The argument developed shows a fine balance that the president must achieve to obtain their goals. To win political support to approve their agenda, the president must form multiparty coalitions and share power with coalition partners. However, to implement their preferences on government policies, the president must retain control over ministries, the main loci of policymaking. Not being able to opt for either of these extremes, the president would strategically allocate ministries.

With a comparative analysis of Brazil, Chile, and Colombia, I tested the president-specific salience argument. The results indicated that the president considers the attributes of the post (salience) and the party characteristics to choose whom to appoint to a ministry. The main conclusions are that the president appoints aligned ministers to policy-relevant cabinet positions to increase control over policymaking. The president also appoints strong coalition parties to policy-relevant cabinet positions to secure legislative support. Last, the president appoints clientelist parties to less relevant posts to achieve both goals of policy control and legislative support. Robustness checks show that these results hold when using alternative measures for salience and alignment. The last contribution was to show how these factors affect the president's choice in each country. This analysis revealed important country-specific patterns that highlight the relevance of the comparative analysis.

Empirically, this work contributes to the literature by presenting innovative measures of portfolio salience. Substantially, the work contributes to the study of political institutions by showing that much still needs to be explained in multiparty presidentialism. Party fragmentation and the minority status of presidents can generate incentives for forming coalition governments in the same way as in parliamentary systems. However, the specific logic of how presidents coalesce shows itself to be different in relation to parliamentarism. Institutional differences matter, and in multiparty presidentialism, the status of natural formateur of the president is key to understanding coalition governance. Presidential discretion, even if constrained, shows at last.

This article aimed to identify how presidents share power in multiparty governments. The argument developed shows a fine balance that the president must achieve to obtain their goals. To win political support to approve their agenda, the president must form multiparty coalitions and share power with coalition partners. However, to implement their preferences on government policies, the president must retain control over ministries, the main loci of policymaking. Not being able to opt for either of these extremes, the president would strategically allocate ministries.
